edges.description: Tick-borne infection seeds bloodstream parasites that invade erythrocytes.
Intraerythrocytic Babesia infection leads to erythrocyte lysis.
Higher parasite burden is associated with hemolysis, coagulopathy, and organ dysfunction.
Hemolysis contributes to severity markers and downstream organ complications.

edges.source: Red blood cell exchange transfusion for severe babesiosis

nodes.description: Atovaquone plus azithromycin is an effective regimen for non-life-threatening babesiosis and causes fewer adverse reactions than clindamycin plus quinine in a randomized trial.

evidence.reference_title: Atovaquone and azithromycin for the treatment of babesiosis.
evidence.snippet: A promising alternative treatment is atovaquone plus azithromycin.

evidence.reference_title: Atovaquone and azithromycin for the treatment of babesiosis.
evidence.snippet: CONCLUSIONS: For the treatment of babesiosis, a regimen of atovaquone and azithromycin is as effective as a regimen of clindamycin and quinine and is associated with fewer adverse reactions.

nodes.description: Babesia parasites invade human erythrocytes and replicate as intraerythrocytic parasites, producing measurable parasitemia.

evidence.snippet: The disease is caused by the protozoa of the genus Babesia, which invade human erythrocytes and lyse them causing a febrile hemolytic anemia.
Blood smear revealed intraerythrocytic Babesia, which was confirmed by PCR.

nodes.description: Erythrocyte lysis by Babesia causes febrile hemolytic anemia and related hematologic abnormalities.

evidence.snippet: The disease is caused by the protozoa of the genus Babesia, which invade human erythrocytes and lyse them causing a febrile hemolytic anemia.

nodes.description: Clindamycin plus quinine is the standard comparator regimen in the non-life-threatening babesiosis trial, but adverse reactions are common.

evidence.reference_title: Atovaquone and azithromycin for the treatment of babesiosis.
evidence.snippet: A course of clindamycin and quinine is the standard treatment, but this regimen frequently causes adverse reactions and occasionally fails.

evidence.reference_title: Atovaquone and azithromycin for the treatment of babesiosis.
evidence.snippet: Adverse effects were reported by 15 percent of the subjects who received atovaquone and azithromycin, as compared with 72 percent of those who received clindamycin and quinine (P<0.001).

nodes.description: Increasing parasite burden is associated with hemolysis, coagulopathy, and pulmonary, renal, and hepatic dysfunction; severe disease is more likely in splenectomized or immunosuppressed patients.

evidence.reference_title: Parasite burden and red blood cell exchange transfusion for babesiosis.
evidence.snippet: Laboratory measures indicating degrees of hemolysis, coagulopathy, and pulmonary, renal and hepatic dysfunction differed significantly across peak parasitemia levels.

evidence.reference_title: Parasite burden and red blood cell exchange transfusion for babesiosis.
evidence.snippet: These results indicate a strong association between peak parasitemia level and disease severity.
follows a persistent, relapsing, and/or life threatening course with multi-organ failure, mainly in the splenectomized or immunosuppressed patients.

nodes.description: Red blood cell exchange transfusion can be considered for severe babesiosis with high parasitemia and end-organ dysfunction.
nodes.id: Red blood cell exchange transfusion for severe babesiosis

evidence.reference_title: Parasite burden and red blood cell exchange transfusion for babesiosis.
evidence.snippet: Nineteen subjects underwent RCE, all with peak parasitemia ≥9% and some degree of end-organ dysfunction.

evidence.reference_title: Parasite burden and red blood cell exchange transfusion for babesiosis.
evidence.snippet: Our data suggest that the use of parasitemia >10%, coupled with clinical status, is a reasonable indicator for RCE in babesiosis patients.

nodes.description: Ixodid ticks introduce Babesia parasites into the human host, initiating bloodstream infection.

evidence.reference_title: Babesia microti: Pathogen Genomics, Genetic Variability, Immunodominant Antigens, and Pathogenesis.
evidence.snippet: Most infections are transmitted by Ixodid (hard-bodied) ticks
